FT_IC1.evidence: Participants were from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, first annual curated baseline data release, 4524 children ages 8-11, 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data using SEM and logistic regression to examine movement behavior recommendations and impulsivity dimensions.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics (2019); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific coefficient (texting, social media, phone time, etc.) appears in any results table or supplementary material. The only screen-time variable reported is a composite ST recommendation indicator (meeting/not-meeting <=2 hrs/day) derived from the 12-item Youth Screen Time Survey. Step 2: The composite includes non-phone/non-SM ingredients per the methods: "watching shows and movies, texting" bundled into a single weighted daily average - TV/movies watching is a disqualifying non-SM ingredient and there is no per-modality breakout anywhere in the paper.

notes: ST composite (Youth Screen Time Survey, 12 items) bundles TV/movies + texting with no per-modality breakout. Only analyses use a binary indicator of meeting the Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines ST recommendation (<=2 hrs/day composite). Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: The cohort is the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study in the Netherlands (n=3102 children aged 5 years, enrolled in Amsterdam 2003-2004). This is the Dutch ABCD cohort, not the U.S. NIH-funded Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Methods explicitly state: "Cross-sectional study including 3102 children aged 5 years from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study, in the Netherlands."

notes: Wrong cohort: Dutch ABCD (Amsterdam Born Children and their Development), not U.S. ABCD Study. Exclusion code: FT-EC1.

FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly uses the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, multi-site longitudinal neuroimaging study, first data release (February 2018), N=4524, 9-10 year-old youth at baseline, 21 U.S. sites, NDA release referenced (doi:10.15154/1412097).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Group Factor Analysis (GFA) relating SMA to cortical thickness, sulcal depth, and gray matter volume, followed by Generalized Linear Mixed Models predicting psychopathology (CBCL internalizing/externalizing) and cognition (fluid/crystallized intelligence) from SMA-related GFA scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in NeuroImage (2019); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient appears in any table or figure. The SMA measure is a total composite score (hours/week) summing all 7 activities: (1) TV/movies, (2) YouTube videos, (3) video games on computer/console/phone/other device, (4) texting, (5) social networking sites, (6) video chat, (7) mature-rated games, (8) R-rated movies. All analyses use either total SMA hours (Table 1) or SMA-related GFA latent scores (Figs. 5-8) derived from the full composite. No per-item breakout coefficient for social media (item 5), texting (item 4), or video chat (item 6) is reported anywhere. Step 2: The composite contains disqualifying non-phone/non-SM ingredients with no separate breakout: TV/movies watching (item 1), video games on console/computer/unspecified platforms (item 3). NOT_MET.

notes: SMA composite bundles TV/movies + console/PC video games + texting + social media + video chat into a single total score. No per-modality breakout with its own coefficient in any analysis. GFA4 noted to load "relatively more on social media" but outcome coefficients are for the latent GFA score, not a social-media-only variable. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.
